region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely adopted in Singapore
food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with protein (meat or vegetable variants)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW represents rice-based composite dishes; used as closest parent as no variant-specific code exists for chicken/lamb/fish/veg biryani.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for biryani as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)
chicken or lamb or fish or mixed vegetables (variant-dependent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of biryani in Singapore is approximately 350 g, typically comprising a generous mound of spiced rice with a protein portion (chicken, lamb, fish) or vegetables, served with raita or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Biryani, chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Biryani
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on the ICMR-NIN chicken biryani entry (cooked, per 100 g edible portion) as the primary anchor, consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for chicken biryani. The record covers multiple protein variants (veg, chicken, lamb, fish); values represent the chicken biryani variant as the nutritional reference case, which is the most commonly documented variant in approved sources. Vegetable biryani would have lower protein and fat; lamb biryani higher fat; fish biryani similar protein with lower saturated fat. Variant-specific values should be recorded separately if required.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; basmati rice-based mixed dish (biryani) analogous estimate anchored to cooked basmati rice GI = 57–58
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for biryani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from cooked basmati rice (GI 57–58, medium category) as the dominant carbohydrate component, per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The addition of protein, fat, and spices in biryani preparation is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice; GI 58 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 43.6, rounded to 43.9 using 21.5 g carb per 100 g × 350 g serving = 75.25 g carb.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of biryani contributes approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid additional salty condiments such as pickles or papadums.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving (~44) due to large rice portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a medium GI driven by basmati rice, the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~75 g carbohydrate) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 44; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce portion size to half a plate, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and meat (~1.8 g per 100 g; ~6.3 g per serving) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The ghee and meat content in biryani contribute saturated fat (~6.3 g per serving) and the sodium load (~1120 mg per serving) is clinically relevant; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the vegetable or fish variant, request preparation with minimal ghee, and limit consumption to occasional meals within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Biryani (also spelled Briyani or Biriyani) is a fragrant, layered rice dish of South Asian origin, widely popular across India, Pakistan, and Singapore. It is prepared by partially cooking long-grain basmati rice separately, then layering it with a spiced protein — typically chicken, lamb (mutton), fish, or mixed vegetables — that has been marinated in yoghurt and aromatic spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. The layered pot is sealed and slow-cooked using the dum method, allowing steam to circulate and infuse the rice with the flavours of the meat and spices. The result is a dish with distinct layers of golden-yellow saffron-tinted rice and richly spiced protein, garnished with fried onions, fresh coriander, and mint. The texture is fluffy yet moist, with a complex aroma profile. In Singapore, biryani is a staple of Indian-Muslim cuisine and is commonly served at hawker centres, Indian restaurants, and catered events, often accompanied by raita (yoghurt condiment), curry sauce, and papadum. Nutritionally, biryani is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant dish with meaningful protein from the meat component. The sodium content is clinically significant due to salt used in cooking and spice blends. The glycemic index is medium (approximately 58), driven by basmati rice, but the glycemic load per standard serving is high due to the large rice portion. Clinically, biryani is a moderate-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, primarily due to its carbohydrate load, sodium content, and saturated fat from ghee and meat. Choosing the fish or vegetable variant and reducing portion size are practical harm-reduction strategies.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of biryani?
What is the difference between chicken biryani and lamb biryani?
Is nasi briyani the same as biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani data cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo; the record covers multiple protein variants under one entry, so per-100g values represent the chicken variant as the reference case. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice data in peer-reviewed international GI tables, not a direct biryani measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. This record covers a multi-variant dish (vegetable, chicken, lamb, fish biryani) sold under one menu item. Nutrient values are anchored to the chicken biryani variant as the best-documented reference in ICMR-NIN and HPB sources. Separate records for each variant are recommended for precision. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or HPB source entries for biryani. 3. Selenium is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. 4. GI of 58 is a conservative analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for biryani was located in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input record listed cuisine as 'Mexican' and cuisine_tags as Mexican/Taco/Burrito/Naan — these are clearly erroneous metadata from the field survey seed record (the restaurants are Indian-Mexican fusion venues). Cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian / South Asian' based on the dish identity. 6. The input food_group 'Meat and alternatives' has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as biryani is a rice-dominant dish. 7. Dietary flag is_vegetarian is set to false as the default reference variant is chicken biryani; the vegetable variant would be vegetarian. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN compositional data for chicken biryani and are approximate.
